metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one test case in `test_fsck_ensures.py` (or a dedicated `test_mcp_startup_ensures.py`) that exercises the MCP startup wiring: verify `run_ensures` is called (e.g., via mock), that an exception from `run_ensures` does NOT raise from `main`, and that no lock is held after the call returns — so AC5 has a concrete in-session proving command.
findings.finding: The MCP server startup AC (AC5) has no described test, no proving command, and is explicitly omitted from the listed scope of `test_fsck_ensures.py`. An executor cannot verify that `run_ensures()` is actually called after `--help` and before `build_server().run()`, that boot continues on error, or that the lock is released before boot — because no test or deterministic check is named anywhere in the plan for this surface.
findings.location: AC5 / 'MCP server startup' section / 'Tests owned by WS3' section
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Tests owned by WS3' section, add an MCP-startup sub-scope: (1) mock `ensures.run_ensures` to raise and assert `mcp_server.main()` continues to `build_server().run()`; (2) mock it to succeed and assert it was called with `attempts=1` and a concrete timeout value; (3) assert it is NOT called when invoked with `--help`. Update AC6 to list these cases.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one test for the MCP startup sweep path in `test_fsck_ensures.py` (or a new `test_mcp_ensures.py`): cover happy-path (sweep called before `build_server().run()`), the log-and-continue contract (exception in `run_ensures` does not raise out of `main`), and that the lock is not held after the startup call.
findings.finding: The MCP server startup sweep is a new DEFAULT behavior (every `mcp_server.main` call now runs `run_ensures()`), but the test plan specifies zero tests for this path. No test verifies the happy path (sweep runs before boot), the short-lock / timeout=N behavior, or the log-and-continue safety contract (ImportError, missing store, sweep error must not abort boot). A new defaulted path with no test coverage can silently break.
findings.location: AC line 5 ('MCP server startup calls run_ensures()…'); 'Tests owned by WS3' section (lists only `test_fsck_ensures.py`, no MCP test)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `tests/interfaces/store/test_mcp_startup_ensures.py` (or extend `test_fsck_ensures.py`) with: (a) a monkeypatched `run_ensures` that raises to assert it never propagates; (b) a monkeypatched `run_ensures` that records when it was called relative to `build_server()` to assert ordering; (c) a test with `timeout=2, attempts=1` that verifies a contended lock skips rather than raises.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a failure-path test to `test_fsck_ensures.py`: mock `run_ensures` to return a `failed` outcome and assert that `fsck --repair` completes without raising, the exit code is unchanged, `issue_count` is not inflated, and the summary line includes the failed unit report.
findings.finding: The `fsck --repair` ensure sweep is a new user-facing flow but the test plan specifies only the happy path (converge + idempotency). No test covers the failure path: a `run_ensures()` that returns one or more `failed` outcomes should NOT abort data-repair, NOT change the exit code, and should still report the failure in the summary line. This is an explicit plan guarantee ('a failed unit… never aborts the data-repair') with no corresponding test.
findings.location: AC line 1 ('NO new mutation flag…'); 'Fold run_ensures() into fsck --repair' section; `test_fsck_ensures.py` scope description
findings.suggested_fix: In `test_fsck_ensures.py`, add a test that patches `ensures.run_ensures` to return `[EnsureOutcome(id='x', status='failed', detail='boom')]` and runs `fsck_cli(..., repair=True)`. Assert return code 0 (or the same as a clean repair), assert `issue_count` unchanged, and assert the output contains 'F failed' or equivalent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC5) to verify that `run_ensures` accepts optional `timeout`/`attempts` kwargs with default `None`, and that the MCP startup path passes a short non-None timeout while existing callers remain unaffected.
findings.finding: The `timeout`/`attempts` passthrough added to `run_ensures` is described as a WS3 deliverable but no AC verifies it — there is no criterion asserting the parameter signature exists, defaults are backward-compatible, or MCP startup exercises a non-default timeout.
findings.location: What section ('MCP server startup') and Scope 'In' list vs Acceptance Criteria

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between Scope Out ('no_mutate-never-sweep behavioral suite → WS5') and AC6 ('WS3 test covers no_mutate-never-sweep') — either remove no_mutate-never-sweep from AC6 and note it is covered by WS5, or move it explicitly into WS3's Scope In list.
findings.finding: AC6 lists `no_mutate`-never-sweep as something WS3's new test file covers, but the Scope section explicitly places `no_mutate-never-sweep` in the Out column (WS5). An implementer cannot determine whether to write that test in WS3 or defer to WS5.
findings.location: Scope section ('Out' list) vs Acceptance Criteria line 6
findings.suggested_fix: In AC6, replace 'and `no_mutate`-never-sweep' with 'and basic `no_mutate`-never-sweeps (the authoritative behavioral suite is WS5)'; and add a matching parenthetical in the Scope Out entry to clarify WS5 owns the authoritative suite while WS3 adds a single integration-level assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the log level and minimum message content for the MCP startup try/except: e.g., log at WARNING with exception type and store path on any non-timeout error, and at DEBUG for an expected skip (missing store or lock timeout), so operators can observe and diagnose sweep failures.
findings.finding: The MCP server startup try/except block is specified to "log-and-continue" on sweep errors, but the plan does not specify the log level, message content, or any structured field that would allow operators to observe and diagnose a failed startup sweep. A silent catch-and-continue makes it impossible to distinguish a healthy skip from an error condition.
findings.location: MCP server startup section — 'Wrap the whole thing in a try/except that log-and-continues'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `timeout=…` with a concrete default value (e.g., `timeout=3`) in the MCP startup description so the executor and any derived test can use it without a design decision.
findings.finding: The `timeout` parameter for `run_ensures` in the MCP startup path is specified as `timeout=…` — a literal placeholder — and described only as 'a few seconds'. An executor must choose an arbitrary value with no stated default, making this a defaultable gap that should be resolved in the plan.
findings.location: 'MCP server startup' section / AC5
findings.suggested_fix: State: 'call `ensures.run_ensures(tracker, timeout=3, attempts=1)` — a 3-second lock budget so a contended lock skips rather than delaying boot.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit prerequisite gate: verify that `from rebar._store.ensures import run_ensures, registry_ids, applied_ids, EnsureOutcome` resolves before WS3 work begins (or reframe the 'WS1 landed' assertion as 'WS1 must land first').
findings.finding: The plan opens with a present-tense confident assertion — 'WS1 landed `rebar._store.ensures` with `run_ensures(tracker) -> list[EnsureOutcome]`, `registry_ids() -> frozenset[str]`, and `applied_ids(tracker) -> set[str]`' — but `src/rebar/_store/` contains no `ensures.py` and none of the named callables exist anywhere in the codebase. Every WS3 integration point (the `--repair` sweep phase, the `_scan` N/M line, and the MCP boot sweep) is contingent on this module, so if it is absent when WS3 executes the plan silently fails with an ImportError at runtime rather than a build-time signal.
findings.location: What section, opening paragraph: 'WS1 landed `rebar._store.ensures` with `run_ensures(tracker) -> list[EnsureOutcome]`...'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the opening to: 'WS3 depends on WS1 landing `rebar._store.ensures`... confirm `src/rebar/_store/ensures.py` exists before starting WS3 implementation.' Add a top-level pre-condition check in the AC or Scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between the fsck.py docstring referencing `tests/interfaces/test_e4_fsck.py` as the byte-parity pin and the plan's assertion that no such test exists: either confirm the file is absent and remove the stale docstring reference, or locate the file and update the plan to account for it.
findings.finding: The plan states 'there is NO existing byte-parity golden for the fsck REPORT output' and 'no golden/byte-parity file needs editing (verified)'. However, `fsck.py`'s own module docstring (line 15) reads: 'Byte-parity pinned by `tests/interfaces/test_e4_fsck.py`'. A search of the entire `tests/` tree finds no `test_e4_fsck.py` anywhere. The plan cites this absent file as evidence that the new `ensures:` line won't break anything, but if the file is genuinely missing it cannot provide that assurance — and if it was deleted the plan's 'verified' claim is untrue.
findings.location: What section, 'Tests owned by WS3': 'there is NO existing byte-parity golden for the fsck REPORT output'; also fsck.py docstring line 15
findings.suggested_fix: Search exhaustively for test_e4_fsck.py; if absent, remove the reference from fsck.py's docstring in the same PR and confirm the plan's 'no golden' claim is correct. If found, add it to the 'tests to verify are unaffected' list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `rebar._store.ensures` (exporting `run_ensures`, `registry_ids`, `applied_ids`, `EnsureOutcome`) is actually merged to origin/main before WS3 begins; add an explicit gate (e.g. `from rebar._store import ensures  # noqa: F401` import check) to the WS3 plan, or declare WS3 depends on WS1's PR merge.
findings.finding: `rebar._store.ensures` (with `run_ensures`, `registry_ids`, `applied_ids`, `EnsureOutcome`) does not exist anywhere in the codebase — `src/rebar/_store/` has no `ensures.py` and none of these symbols appear in any source file. The plan's 'What' section presents these as already landed by WS1, but WS3 cannot be implemented without them.
findings.location: What section — 'WS1 landed `rebar._store.ensures` with `run_ensures(tracker) -> list[EnsureOutcome]`, `registry_ids() -> frozenset[str]`, and `applied_ids(tracker) -> set[str]`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Prerequisites' section to the WS3 plan: 'WS1's PR must be merged to origin/main before WS3 implementation begins. Verify with: `python -c "from rebar._store import ensures; print(ensures.registry_ids())"`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the discrepancy: either confirm `tests/interfaces/test_e4_fsck.py` does not exist (and update `fsck.py`'s docstring to remove the stale reference), or document where the byte-parity pin actually lives so WS3 implementers know the real constraint.
findings.finding: `tests/interfaces/test_e4_fsck.py` — cited in `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py`'s own module docstring as the byte-parity pin file — does not exist in the repository. The plan states 'There is NO existing byte-parity golden for the fsck REPORT output' and says 'verified: rerun test_cli_argparse stays green', but this specific file is cited in fsck.py itself and is absent, making the 'no byte-parity golden' claim unverifiable and potentially incorrect.
findings.location: What section — 'Tests owned by WS3' paragraph; also `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py` line 15: 'Byte-parity pinned by `tests/interfaces/test_e4_fsck.py`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate and verify the three unenumerated `fsck_cli` callers (`_lib_reads.py:274`, `test_compact_negative.py:94`, `test_tracker_config_e2e.py:113`) to confirm none asserts on fsck output content that would be broken by the new `ensures: N/M applied` line; add a note to the plan if they are resilient.
findings.finding: Three callers of `fsck_cli` outside `src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py` — `src/rebar/_lib_reads.py:274`, `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_compact_negative.py:94`, and `tests/interfaces/store/test_tracker_config_e2e.py:113` — are not enumerated in the plan. All three will observe the new `ensures: N/M applied` line in `_scan()` output (since `_scan` is the shared read-only path). The plan asserts the line is additive and does not break any existing test, but does not name these consumers or confirm they are resilient to the new output line.
findings.location: What section — 'Read-only `ensures: N/M applied` line' and 'Tests owned by WS3' paragraphs
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Tests owned by WS3' paragraph: 'Confirmed safe: `_lib_reads.py` returns the raw output string (callers assert only on return code); `test_compact_negative.py` only checks `rc`; `test_tracker_config_e2e.py` only asserts `rc == 0`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `rebar._store.ensures` (with `run_ensures`, `registry_ids`, `applied_ids`, `EnsureOutcome`) is present and importable in the branch WS3 targets, or add an explicit WS1-merge prerequisite gate to the plan so executors know WS3 cannot start until that module exists.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'WS1 landed rebar._store.ensures with run_ensures(tracker)->list[EnsureOutcome], registry_ids()->frozenset[str], and applied_ids(tracker)->set[str]' as a verified fact, but src/rebar/_store/ contains no ensures.py and no search hit for run_ensures, EnsureOutcome, registry_ids, or applied_ids exists anywhere in src/. WS3's entire implementation — the _scan() N/M line, the --repair sweep phase, and the MCP startup call — depends on this API surface being callable at import time. If WS1 has not actually landed, WS3 will fail at the first import.
findings.location: What section, opening paragraph: 'WS1 landed rebar._store.ensures with run_ensures(tracker) -> list[EnsureOutcome], registry_ids() -> frozenset[str], and applied_ids(tracker) -> set[str]'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line prerequisite block: 'Prerequisite: WS1 must be merged and `from rebar._store import ensures; ensures.registry_ids()` must succeed before any WS3 work begins.' Optionally add a CI guard: `python -c 'from rebar._store.ensures import run_ensures, registry_ids, applied_ids, EnsureOutcome'` as a pre-flight check in the WS3 test suite.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `rebar._store.ensures` (exporting `run_ensures`, `registry_ids`, `applied_ids`, `EnsureOutcome`) is merged and importable before WS3 work begins; add an explicit WS1→WS3 sequencing gate or stub/interface contract so WS3 integration can be verified independently.
findings.finding: `rebar._store.ensures` (with `run_ensures`, `registry_ids`, `applied_ids`, `EnsureOutcome`) does not exist in `src/rebar/_store/` — the module the entire WS3 integration depends on is absent from the codebase. The plan asserts 'WS1 landed `rebar._store.ensures`' but no `ensures.py` (or equivalent) is present in the verified store directory.
findings.location: What section, opening paragraph: 'WS1 landed `rebar._store.ensures` with `run_ensures(tracker) -> list[EnsureOutcome]`, `registry_ids() -> frozenset[str]`, and `applied_ids(tracker) -> set[str]`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sequencing precondition note: 'WS3 MUST NOT begin until `rebar._store.ensures` is importable from the working branch. Verify with `python -c "from rebar._store import ensures; ensures.registry_ids()"`.' Alternatively, define a minimal interface (Protocol or abstract stub) for the three callables so WS3 integration tests can run against a WS1 stub.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document an explicit back-out path for the `fsck --repair` behavioral change — e.g., confirm that `--dry-run --repair` is the escape hatch, or add a note that a `--skip-ensures` flag could be introduced if rollback is needed, or describe how to revert to a prior version; acknowledgement alone is not sufficient per T4.
findings.finding: The plan acknowledges that existing `fsck --repair` callers now also converge config (a behavioral change) but provides no rollback path, back-out plan, or expand-contract sequence — acknowledgement alone does not satisfy T4's reversibility requirement.
findings.location: What section, paragraph 1: 'This means existing `fsck --repair` callers now ALSO converge config — an acknowledged behavioral change.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the What section such as: 'Back-out: operators who need to suppress the ensure sweep can run `rebar fsck --repair --dry-run` (which does not sweep by design) or pin to the prior release; a `--skip-ensures` flag is out-of-scope but can be added as a follow-on if operational experience warrants it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief entry to CLAUDE.md (or an inline ADR comment in `fsck.py` and `mcp_server.main`) documenting the architectural decision to fold config-convergence (ensures sweep) into `fsck --repair` and MCP boot, and explaining why it is intentionally a side-effect of the data-repair verb rather than a separate flag.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a deliberate behavioral change to existing `fsck --repair` callers (they now also converge config/ensures as a side effect) and a new cross-component wiring pattern (mcp_server → ensures at boot), but no ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design-doc update is mentioned to capture this architectural decision for future maintainers.
findings.location: What section — 'Fold run_ensures() into fsck --repair' + 'MCP server startup'; also Scope paragraph ('acknowledged behavioral change')
findings.suggested_fix: In `fsck.py` near the `if do_repair:` block, add a block comment: `# WS3: config-convergence (ensures sweep) is intentionally folded here — see docs/adr/NNN-ensures-sweep.md`. Add a parallel note in `mcp_server.main`. Capture the decision (why folded, not a separate flag, acknowledged behavioral change) in a short ADR or CLAUDE.md section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a RETIRE/compaction path for the `.ensure-applied` marker: specify when (e.g., during `run_ensures` or `fsck --repair`) stale IDs (present in the marker but absent from `registry_ids()`) are pruned from the file, so the marker doesn't accumulate entries indefinitely.
findings.finding: The `.ensure-applied` marker file has no defined retirement/cleanup path: as ensures are registered and later deregistered over a store's lifetime, old ensure IDs accumulate in the marker file indefinitely. The plan mitigates inflation of the displayed N via the `applied_ids & registry_ids()` intersection, but the underlying file grows unboundedly and stale IDs are never pruned.
findings.location: What section — 'Read-only ensures: N/M applied' and Acceptance Criteria AC3
findings.suggested_fix: During the `run_ensures` sweep, after applying all registered ensures, write back the marker containing only `registry_ids()` entries that are now applied — effectively pruning any IDs no longer in the registry. Since run_ensures already holds the write lock at that point, this is safe and atomic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and test the M=0 (empty registry) case for the `ensures: N/M applied` line: either suppress the line entirely when M=0, or display an alternative message (e.g., `ensures: none registered`), and confirm the repair-hint suppression (`N<M` → false when both are 0) is intentional.
findings.finding: The `ensures: N/M applied` read-only line does not address the M=0 edge case (empty ensure registry). Displaying `ensures: 0/0 applied` could confuse users who see a fraction with a zero denominator, and the ` — run rebar fsck --repair to converge` suffix logic (`N < M`) would suppress the hint (0 < 0 is false), which may be the right behavior but is unspecified.
findings.location: What section — 'Read-only ensures: N/M applied line', AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `timeout`/`attempts` need to be on `run_ensures`'s public signature, or whether MCP startup's short-lock requirement can be met by an internal named constant inside `run_ensures` (e.g. a `_MCP_LOCK_TIMEOUT` literal), keeping the public API stable and avoiding a Rule-of-Three violation.
findings.finding: The `timeout`/`attempts` passthrough added to `run_ensures`'s public signature has only one actual override user (MCP startup); `fsck --repair` calls it with defaults. This parameterises the WS1 API for a single call-site, violating Rule-of-Three for the added API surface. The existing `write_lock` already accepts `timeout`/`attempts` natively, so `run_ensures` could simply pass a different internal constant (e.g. `_MCP_LOCK_TIMEOUT = 3`) without exposing the knobs on its public signature.
findings.location: What section — MCP server startup paragraph: 'WS3 adds an optional `timeout`/`attempts` passthrough to `run_ensures` that forwards to `write_lock`; default `None` keeps WS1's behavior unchanged.'
findings.suggested_fix: Define a private `_ENSURE_BOOT_TIMEOUT = 3` constant inside `run_ensures` (or `mcp_server.py`) and pass it directly to `write_lock` inside `run_ensures` when the caller requests best-effort mode via a boolean flag (e.g. `best_effort: bool = False`) rather than threading raw lock parameters through the public API. This keeps the lock knobs encapsulated and avoids the premature API surface.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `.ensure-applied` marker writes are atomic (write to a temp file then `os.replace`/rename into place), AND assert that individual ensure units registered in the registry must be idempotent (re-applying an already-applied unit is a safe no-op) so that a partial-write recovery via re-sweep is guaranteed safe.
findings.finding: The plan never specifies whether `.ensure-applied` is written atomically (e.g., via a temp-file + rename) or in-place. A non-atomic write killed mid-write produces a corrupt/partial file; per the plan's own degradation rule this resets to `applied_ids == set()`, forcing a full re-sweep. Re-sweep safety depends entirely on individual ensure units being idempotent at the filesystem/side-effect level — but the plan never asserts or requires unit-level idempotency. An ensure unit that is NOT idempotent (e.g., one that appends to a file rather than replacing it) would double-apply on re-run after a partial-write failure.
findings.location: What section — 'Read-only ensures: N/M line' + AC2 (idempotency); the `run_ensures` description in WS1 assumed context
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `run_ensures` contract in WS1/WS3: 'marker writes MUST use `os.replace` (atomic rename) to avoid partial-write corruption; all registered ensure units MUST be idempotent (applying a unit whose effect is already present is a no-op).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the schema state of `.ensure-applied` after a partial `run_ensures()` failure and after a data-repair git rollback (e.g., 'marker is left as-is; re-running `fsck --repair` re-applies any unapplied units idempotently'), and document whether operators need to manually delete the marker to force a full re-sweep after rolling back via the pre-a3-remediation tag.
findings.finding: The ensure sweep phase inside `fsck --repair` has no described rollback or back-out path. The data-repair phase (`_repair_run`) explicitly creates a `pre-a3-remediation` git tag for rollback. If `run_ensures()` fully or partially writes `.ensure-applied` and then aborts (or a unit leaves the store in an intermediate state), the plan provides no mechanism to undo or verify the ensure-phase side-effects. The plan says failed units are 'log-and-continue' but does not assert what the schema state of `.ensure-applied` is after a partial sweep failure, nor whether it is safe to leave the marker in that state.
findings.location: What section — 'Fold `run_ensures()` into `fsck --repair`'; AC1; AC2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a formal enumeration of `EnsureOutcome`'s exhaustive status vocabulary (`ok`, `changed`, `failed` — or whatever the canonical set is) to this plan, co-located with the report-format string in the What section, so implementers and test authors have a single authoritative reference.
findings.finding: `EnsureOutcome`'s exhaustive value vocabulary is referenced but never formally defined in this plan. The plan's report-format string (`ensures: swept N unit(s); C changed, F failed`) and the fallback description (`run_ensures` → `failed`) imply exactly three status values — `ok`, `changed`, `failed` — but these are never enumerated as an authoritative specification. The host module `rebar._store.ensures` does not exist in the codebase (confirmed: no matches for `EnsureOutcome`, `run_ensures`, `registry_ids`, or `applied_ids` under `src/`), so WS1 has not yet landed the definition, and this plan provides no enum definition itself.
findings.location: What section — 'WS1 landed `rebar._store.ensures` with `run_ensures(tracker) -> list[EnsureOutcome]`'; report-format string 'ensures: swept N unit(s); C changed, F failed'; AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What / Fold run_ensures() into fsck --repair' section: 'EnsureOutcome is a dataclass/namedtuple with fields `unit_id: str` and `status: Literal["ok", "changed", "failed"]`; `ok` = already applied (no-op), `changed` = newly applied, `failed` = sweep attempted but errored. The summary counts: N = total units swept, C = count(status=="changed"), F = count(status=="failed").'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
